title: The Palace Of Art
content:
I built my soul a lordly pleasure-house,
Wherein at ease for aye to dwell.
I said, ï¿½O Soul, make merry and carouse,
Dear soul, for all is well.ï¿½

A huge crag-platform, smooth as burnishï¿½d brass
I chose. The ranged ramparts bright
From level meadow-bases of deep grass
Suddenly scaled the light.

Thereon I built it firm. Of ledge or shelf
The rock rose clear, or winding stair.
My soul would live alone unto herself
In her high palace there.

And ï¿½while the world runs round and round,ï¿½ I said,
ï¿½Reign thou apart, a quiet king,
Still as, while Saturn whirls, his steadfast shade
Sleeps on his luminous ring.ï¿½

To which my soul made answer readily:
ï¿½Trust me, in bliss I shall abide
In this great mansion, that is built for me,
So royal-rich and wide.ï¿½

* * * * *

Four courts I made, East, West and South and North,
In each a squared lawn, wherefrom
The golden gorge of dragons spouted forth
A flood of fountain-foam.

And round the cool green courts there ran a row
Of cloisters, branchï¿½d like mighty woods,
Echoing all night to that sonorous flow
Of spouted fountain-floods.

And round the roofs a gilded gallery
That lent broad verge to distant lands,
Far as the wild swan wings, to where the sky
Dipt down to sea and sands.

From those four jets four currents in one swell
Across the mountain streamï¿½d below
In misty folds, that floating as they fell
Lit up a torrent-bow.

And high on every peak a statue seemï¿½d
To hang on tiptoe, tossing up
A cloud of incense of all odour steamï¿½d
From out a golden cup.

So that she thought, ï¿½And who shall gaze upon
My palace with unblinded eyes,
While this great bow will waver in the sun,
And that sweet incense rise?ï¿½

For that sweet incense rose and never failï¿½d,
And, while day sank or mounted higher,
The light aï¿½rial gallery, golden-railï¿½d,
Burnt like a fringe of fire.

Likewise the deep-set windows, stainï¿½d and traced,
Would seem slow-flaming crimson fires
From shadowï¿½d grots of arches interlaced,
And tipt with frost-like spires.

* * * * *

Full of long-sounding corridors it was,
That over-vaulted grateful gloom,
Throï¿½ which the livelong day my soul did pass,
Well-pleased, from room to room.

Full of great rooms and small the palace stood,
All various, each a perfect whole
From living Nature, fit for every mood
And change of my still soul.

For some were hung with arras green and blue,
Showing a gaudy summer-morn,
Where with puffï¿½d cheek the belted hunter blew
His wreathed bugle-horn.

One seemï¿½d all dark and redï¿½a tract of sand,
And some one pacing there alone,
Who paced for ever in a glimmering land,
Lit with a low large moon.

One showï¿½d an iron coast and angry waves.
You seemï¿½d to hear them climb and fall
And roar rock-thwarted under bellowing caves,
Beneath the windy wall.

And one, a full-fed river winding slow
By herds upon an endless plain,
The ragged rims of thunder brooding low,
With shadow-streaks of rain.

And one, the reapers at their sultry toil.
In front they bound the sheaves. Behind
Were realms of upland, prodigal in oil,
And hoary to the wind.

And one a foreground black with stones and slags,
Beyond, a line of heights, and higher
All barrï¿½d with long white cloud the scornful crags,
And highest, snow and fire.

And one, an English homeï¿½gray twilight pourï¿½d
On dewy pastures, dewy trees,
Softer than sleepï¿½all things in order stored,
A haunt of ancient Peace.

Nor these alone, but every landscape fair,
As fit for every mood of mind,
Or gay, or grave, or sweet, or stern, was there,
Not less than truth designï¿½d.

* * * * *

Or the maid-mother by a crucifix,
In tracts of pasture sunny-warm,
Beneath branch-work of costly sardonyx
Sat smiling, babe in arm.

Or in a clear-wallï¿½d city on the sea,
Near gilded organ-pipes, her hair
Wound with white roses, slept St. Cecily;
An angel lookï¿½d at her.

Or thronging all one porch of Paradise
A group of Houris bowï¿½d to see
The dying Islamite, with hands and eyes
That said, We wait for thee.

Or mythic Utherï¿½s deeply-wounded son
In some fair space of sloping greens
Lay, dozing in the vale of Avalon,
And watchï¿½d by weeping queens.

Or hollowing one hand against his ear,
To list a foot-fall, ere he saw
The wood-nymph, stayï¿½d the Ausonian king to hear
Of wisdom and of law.

Or over hills with peaky tops engrailï¿½d,
And many a tract of palm and rice,
The throne of Indian Cama slowly sailï¿½d
A summer fannï¿½d with spice.

Or sweet Europaï¿½s mantle blew unclaspï¿½d,
From off her shoulder backward borne:
From one hand droopï¿½d a crocus: one hand graspï¿½d
The mild bullï¿½s golden horn.

Or else flushï¿½d Ganymede, his rosy thigh
Half-buried in the Eagleï¿½s down,
Sole as a flying star shot throï¿½ the sky
Above the pillarï¿½d town.

Nor these alone: but every legend fair
Which the supreme Caucasian mind
Carved out of Nature for itself, was there,
Not less than life, designï¿½d.

* * * * *

Then in the towers I placed great bells that swung,
Moved of themselves, with silver sound;
And with choice paintings of wise men I hung
The royal dais round.

For there was Milton like a seraph strong,
Beside him Shakespeare bland and mild;
And there the world-worn Dante graspï¿½d his song,
And somewhat grimly smiled.

And there the Ionian father of the rest;
A million wrinkles carved his skin;
A hundred winters snowï¿½d upon his breast,
From cheek and throat and chin.

Above, the fair hall-ceiling stately-set
Many an arch high up did lift,
And angels rising and descending met
With interchange of gift.

Below was all mosaic choicely plannï¿½d
With cycles of the human tale
Of this wide world, the times of every land
So wrought, they will not fail.

The people here, a beast of burden slow,
Toilï¿½d onward, prickï¿½d with goads and stings;
Here playï¿½d, a tiger, rolling to and fro
The heads and crowns of kings;

Here rose, an athlete, strong to break or bind
All force in bonds that might endure,
And here once more like some sick man declined,
And trusted any cure.

But over these she trod: and those great bells
Began to chime. She took her throne:
She sat betwixt the shining Oriels,
To sing her songs alone.

And throï¿½ the topmost Orielsï¿½ coloured flame
Two godlike faces gazed below;
Plato the wise, and large-browï¿½d Verulam,
The first of those who know.

And all those names, that in their motion were
Full-welling fountain-heads of change,
Betwixt the slender shafts were blazonï¿½d fair
In diverse raiment strange:

Throï¿½ which the lights, rose, amber, emerald, blue,
Flushï¿½d in her temples and her eyes,
And from her lips, as morn from Memnon, drew
Rivers of melodies.

No nightingale delighteth to prolong
Her low preamble all alone,
More than my soul to hear her echoï¿½d song
Throb throï¿½ the ribbed stone;

Singing and murmuring in her feastful mirth,
Joying to feel herself alive,
Lord over Nature, Lord of the visible earth,
Lord of the senses five;

Communing with herself: ï¿½All these are mine,
And let the world have peace or wars,
ï¿½Tis one to me.ï¿½ Sheï¿½when young night divine
Crownï¿½d dying day with stars,

Making sweet close of his delicious toilsï¿½
Lit light in wreaths and anadems,
And pure quintessences of precious oils
In hollowï¿½d moons of gems,

To mimic heaven; and clapt her hands and cried,
ï¿½I marvel if my still delight
In this great house so royal-rich, and wide,
Be flatterï¿½d to the height.

ï¿½O all things fair to sate my various eyes!
O shapes and hues that please me well!
O silent faces of the Great and Wise,
My Gods, with whom I dwell!

ï¿½O God-like isolation which art mine,
I can but count thee perfect gain,
What time I watch the darkening droves of swine
That range on yonder plain.

ï¿½In filthy sloughs they roll a prurient skin,
They graze and wallow, breed and sleep;
And oft some brainless devil enters in,
And drives them to the deep.ï¿½

Then of the moral instinct would she prate
And of the rising from the dead,
As hers by right of full-accomplishï¿½d Fate;
And at the last she said:

ï¿½I take possession of manï¿½s mind and deed.
I care not what the sects may brawl.
I sit as God holding no form of creed,
But contemplating all.ï¿½

* * * * *

Full oft the riddle of the painful earth
Flashï¿½d throï¿½ her as she sat alone,
Yet not the less held she her solemn mirth,
And intellectual throne.

And so she throve and prosperï¿½d: so three years

She prosperï¿½d: on the fourth she fell,
Like Herod, when the shout was in his ears,
Struck throï¿½ with pangs of hell.

Lest she should fail and perish utterly,
God, before whom ever lie bare
The abysmal deeps of Personality,
Plagued her with sore despair.

When she would think, whereï¿½er she turnï¿½d her sight
The airy hand confusion wrought,
Wrote, ï¿½Mene, mene,ï¿½ and divided quite
The kingdom of her thought.

Deep dread and loathing of her solitude
Fell on her, from which mood was born
Scorn of herself; again, from out that mood
Laughter at her self-scorn.

ï¿½What! is not this my place of strength,ï¿½ she said,
ï¿½My spacious mansion built for me,
Whereof the strong foundation-stones were laid
Since my first memory?ï¿½

But in dark corners of her palace stood
Uncertain shapes; and unawares
On white-eyed phantasms weeping tears of blood,
And horrible nightmares,

And hollow shades enclosing hearts of flame,
And, with dim fretted foreheads all,
On corpses three-months-old at noon she came,
That stood against the wall.

A spot of dull stagnation, without light
Or power of movement, seemï¿½d my soul,
ï¿½Mid onward-sloping motions infinite
Making for one sure goal.

A still salt pool, lockï¿½d in with bars of sand,
Left on the shore; that hears all night
The plunging seas draw backward from the land
Their moon-led waters white.

A star that with the choral starry dance
Joinï¿½d not, but stood, and standing saw
The hollow orb of moving Circumstance
Rollï¿½d round by one fixï¿½d law.

Back on herself her serpent pride had curlï¿½d.
ï¿½No voice,ï¿½ she shriekï¿½d in that lone hall,
ï¿½No voice breaks throï¿½ the stillness of this world:
One deep, deep silence all!ï¿½

She, mouldering with the dull earthï¿½s mouldering sod,
Inwrapt tenfold in slothful shame,
Lay there exiled from eternal God,
Lost to her place and name;

And death and life she hated equally,
And nothing saw, for her despair,
But dreadful time, dreadful eternity,
No comfort anywhere;

Remaining utterly confused with fears,
And ever worse with growing time,
And ever unrelieved by dismal tears,
And all alone in crime:

Shut up as in a crumbling tomb, girt round
With blackness as a solid wall,
Far off she seemï¿½d to hear the dully sound
Of human footsteps fall.

As in strange lands a traveller walking slow,
In doubt and great perplexity,
A little before moon-rise hears the low
Moan of an unknown sea;

And knows not if it be thunder, or a sound
Of rocks thrown down, or one deep cry
Of great wild beasts; then thinketh, ï¿½I have found
A new land, but I die.ï¿½

She howlï¿½d aloud, ï¿½I am on fire within.
There comes no murmur of reply.
What is it that will take away my sin,
And save me lest I die?ï¿½

So when four years were wholly finished,
She threw her royal robes away.
ï¿½Make me a cottage in the vale,ï¿½ she said,
ï¿½Where I may mourn and pray.

ï¿½Yet pull not down my palace towers, that are
So lightly, beautifully built;
Perchance I may return with others there
When I have purged my guilt.ï¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
